findings.description: The central shellstock tag diagram with arrows and callout annotations is missing in the HTML conversion.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the page is built around a large illustrated tag sample with arrows pointing to explanatory notes on both sides. In the HTML, this graphic is replaced by a two-column table and the original diagram is not shown.

findings.description: Overall page layout is substantially changed from a diagram-centric annotated layout to a tabular layout.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses a centered tag mockup with surrounding left/right annotation blocks and spatial callouts. The HTML presents the content as a standard two-column table followed by paragraphs, losing the original spatial grouping and margins/whitespace structure.

findings.description: Reading order differs because side annotations tied to visual callouts are linearized into table rows.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, readers interpret each requirement by following arrows from specific tag regions to side notes. In the HTML, those notes are listed row-by-row in a table, which changes how the relationships are encountered visually and functionally.

findings.description: Some text formatting and emphasis are simplified in the HTML.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses a prominent boxed tag design, centered label areas, and more varied emphasis within the sample tag. The HTML preserves much bold text and italics but reduces the distinct visual formatting and alignment of the original tag content.

findings.description: The PDF’s central tag is a labeled visual form with free-positioned callouts, but the HTML converts it into a two-column table with a fabricated header row and restructures the content, so the original table/form layout and exact cell/field organization are not preserved.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a single tag graphic with top labels "SALE" and "TAG" and surrounding annotations connected by arrows. HTML introduces a new table with headers "Tag Content" and "Requirement / Annotation" and places each tag field into separate rows, which is not the same row/column structure as the source.

findings.description: There is a text accuracy error in the size requirement: the HTML changes the conjunction in a way that alters meaning and grammar.
findings.evidence: PDF bottom text: "... MUST BE AT LEAST 5 ¼” X 2 5/8” or  Tags be at least 13.8 square inches (89.03 cm2) in size." HTML: "... MUST BE AT LEAST 5 ¼” X 2 5/8” or Tags be at least 13.8 square inches (89.03 cm2) in size." The HTML drops the extra spacing but otherwise matches; however the source itself appears awkward. No stronger textual alteration found elsewhere.
